keyConcepts.label: The Prisoner's Dilemma
keyConcepts.microLesson: The **Prisoner's Dilemma** is the most famous game: each player chooses **Cooperate** or **Defect**, with payoffs ordered $T > R > P > S$ (Temptation > Reward > Punishment > Sucker). Because $T > R$ and $P > S$, **Defect strictly dominates**, so the unique Nash equilibrium is (Defect, Defect). Yet both would be better off at (Cooperate, Cooperate) — a **Pareto-superior** outcome that individual rationality destroys. The dilemma: self-interested play makes everyone worse off. It models arms races, price wars, overfishing, and doping.

keyConcepts.label: Coordination games
keyConcepts.microLesson: **Coordination games** reward matching. In the **Stag Hunt**, two hunters get a big reward only if both choose Stag; either can safely take a small Hare alone. Both (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) are Nash equilibria — (Stag, Stag) is **payoff-dominant** (best for both) but risky; (Hare, Hare) is **risk-dominant** (safe). In the **Battle of the Sexes**, both players want to be together but disagree on where, again giving two equilibria. The strategic problem is selecting *which* equilibrium — trust, communication, or a focal point decides it.

keyConcepts.label: Anti-coordination games
keyConcepts.microLesson: **Anti-coordination games** reward mismatching. In **Chicken**, two drivers head at each other; each wants to go Straight only if the other Swerves, and mutual Straight is a disastrous crash. In **Hawk-Dove** (its biology twin), animals contest a resource worth $V$ at fighting cost $C > V$; a Hawk beats a Dove but two Hawks bloody each other. The two pure equilibria are **off-diagonal** — exactly one player is aggressive — and each prefers to be the aggressive one. There is also a mixed equilibrium giving the stable Hawk fraction in a population.

keyConcepts.label: Matching Pennies and zero-sum
keyConcepts.microLesson: **Matching Pennies** is the canonical **zero-sum** game: one player wins exactly what the other loses (payoffs sum to 0 in every cell). Row wins by matching, Column by mismatching, so from any cell someone wants to deviate — there is **no pure Nash equilibrium**. The unique equilibrium is **mixed**: by symmetry each plays each option with probability $\tfrac12$, and the value of the game is 0 (fair). Zero-sum games are the setting of von Neumann's minimax theorem and model pure competition — pursuit, penalty kicks, and inspection.

lecture.body:
## Four games that explain half the world

A remarkable fact about $2\times2$ games is that a handful of them recur everywhere — in economics, biology, politics, and daily life. Learn these four archetypes and you will recognize their skeleton inside a startling range of real conflicts.

## 1. The Prisoner's Dilemma — cooperation's tragedy

Two suspects are interrogated separately. Each can **Cooperate** (stay silent, loyal to the other) or **Defect** (confess, betray). A typical payoff table (higher = better for that player):

| Row \ Col | Cooperate | Defect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooperate** | 3, 3 | 0, 5 |
| **Defect** | 5, 0 | 1, 1 |

The four payoffs obey the defining order $T(5) > R(3) > P(1) > S(0)$: **Temptation** to defect on a cooperator, **Reward** for mutual cooperation, **Punishment** for mutual defection, **Sucker** for cooperating against a defector.

**Solve it.** For Row, Defect beats Cooperate against Cooperate ($5 > 3$) *and* against Defect ($1 > 0$): **Defect strictly dominates**. Same for Column. So the unique Nash equilibrium is $(\text{Defect}, \text{Defect})$ at $(1, 1)$.

**The dilemma.** Both would rather be at $(\text{Cooperate}, \text{Cooperate}) = (3, 3)$ — a **Pareto improvement** of $2$ each — but neither can get there unilaterally, and each is tempted to defect. Rational self-interest yields a collectively worse result. This is the logic of arms races, price wars, overfishing, climate free-riding, and doping in sports: everyone defects, everyone loses.

## 2. Coordination games — the problem of agreeing

### Stag Hunt

Two hunters can bag a **Stag** (a feast) only if *both* commit; either can instead catch a **Hare** alone (a snack) regardless of the other.

| Row \ Col | Stag | Hare |
|---|---|---|
| **Stag** | 5, 5 | 0, 3 |
| **Hare** | 3, 0 | 3, 3 |

Best-response analysis gives **two** pure equilibria: $(\text{Stag}, \text{Stag})$ and $(\text{Hare}, \text{Hare})$. $(\text{Stag}, \text{Stag})$ is **payoff-dominant** — best for both at $5$ each — but risky: if your partner flakes, Stag earns $0$. $(\text{Hare}, \text{Hare})$ is **risk-dominant** — Hare guarantees $3$ no matter what. Whether players reach the better equilibrium is a question of **trust**.

### Battle of the Sexes

A couple wants an evening **together**, but Row prefers Opera and Column prefers Boxing; being apart is worst for both.

| Row \ Col | Opera | Boxing |
|---|---|---|
| **Opera** | 3, 2 | 0, 0 |
| **Boxing** | 0, 0 | 2, 3 |

Again **two** equilibria, $(\text{Opera}, \text{Opera})$ and $(\text{Boxing}, \text{Boxing})$ — the players agree they want to coordinate but **disagree on which** equilibrium. Communication, convention, or a focal point ("we always do Opera on Fridays") resolves it.

## 3. Anti-coordination — Chicken and Hawk-Dove

### Chicken

Two drivers speed toward each other. **Swerve** or go **Straight**? If both go Straight they crash (worst); if one swerves, the "chicken" loses face while the other wins.

| Row \ Col | Swerve | Straight |
|---|---|---|
| **Swerve** | 3, 3 | 2, 4 |
| **Straight** | 4, 2 | 1, 1 |

The pure equilibria are the two **off-diagonal** cells, $(\text{Swerve}, \text{Straight})$ and $(\text{Straight}, \text{Swerve})$: exactly one driver yields, and each *prefers to be the one who doesn't*. Mutual Straight is not an equilibrium — from a crash either would rather have swerved. Chicken models brinkmanship, from labor strikes to the Cuban Missile Crisis.

### Hawk-Dove (the biology version)

Animals contest a resource worth $V$. Playing **Hawk** (fight) against a **Dove** (yield) wins the whole $V$; two Doves split it $V/2$ each; two Hawks fight at cost $C$ and each nets $(V - C)/2$, which is negative when $C > V$. Same off-diagonal equilibria — and a **mixed** equilibrium giving the stable fraction of aggressive individuals in a population, a founding result of evolutionary game theory.

## 4. Matching Pennies — pure competition, zero-sum

| Row \ Col | Heads | Tails |
|---|---|---|
| **Heads** | 1, -1 | -1, 1 |
| **Tails** | -1, 1 | 1, -1 |

Every cell sums to $0$: this is a **zero-sum** game, pure conflict, one's gain is the other's loss. Row wins by matching, Column by mismatching, so best responses cycle and there is **no pure equilibrium**. By symmetry the unique equilibrium is **mixed**, each side playing $\tfrac12$–$\tfrac12$, with game **value $0$** — a fair fight. Zero-sum games are where **von Neumann's minimax theorem** (1928) lives: each player can guarantee the value by choosing the mix that minimizes their maximum loss. Poker, pursuit, and penalty kicks all live here.

### How to tell them apart

| Game | Equilibria | Signature |
|---|---|---|
| Prisoner's Dilemma | 1 pure (Defect, Defect) | dominance; equilibrium is Pareto-inferior |
| Coordination (Stag Hunt / BoS) | 2 pure, on the diagonal | reward matching; selection problem |
| Anti-coordination (Chicken / Hawk-Dove) | 2 pure, off the diagonal | reward mismatching; each wants to be aggressor |
| Matching Pennies | 0 pure, 1 mixed | zero-sum; must randomize |

Spotting which archetype a real situation matches is often 80% of the strategic insight.

application.summary: The four classic 2×2 games are a diagnostic toolkit: identify whether a real conflict is a Prisoner's Dilemma (cooperation collapses), a coordination game (which equilibrium?), an anti-coordination game (who yields?), or zero-sum (randomize) — and the right intervention (contracts, communication, commitment, unpredictability) follows immediately.
application.workedExample:
**Two nations and an arms race — a Prisoner's Dilemma.**

Each nation chooses to **Disarm** (Cooperate) or **Arm** (Defect). Arming is costly and dangerous but leaves you vulnerable if you disarm while the rival arms. Payoffs (security & prosperity):

| Row \ Col | Disarm | Arm |
|---|---|---|
| **Disarm** | 3, 3 | 0, 5 |
| **Arm** | 5, 0 | 1, 1 |

*Step 1 — find the dominant strategy.* For Row, Arm beats Disarm against Disarm ($5 > 3$) and against Arm ($1 > 0$). **Arm strictly dominates**; same for Column.

*Step 2 — the equilibrium.* $(\text{Arm}, \text{Arm})$ at $(1, 1)$: both nations bristling with weapons, poorer and no safer.

*Step 3 — the tragedy.* $(\text{Disarm}, \text{Disarm}) = (3, 3)$ is a Pareto improvement of $2$ each, but unreachable without enforcement. Real arms-control **treaties, inspections, and verification** are institutions designed to change the payoffs — punishing defection so cooperation becomes an equilibrium. That is exactly how repeated play and enforcement (later topics) rescue cooperation from the dilemma.

*Contrast:* if instead the situation rewarded doing the *opposite* of the rival (a regional power vacuum only one can fill), it would be **Chicken**, and the advice flips from "build enforcement" to "credibly commit / don't blink." Diagnosing the archetype is the whole game.
domainNotes: **Antitrust:** cartels are Prisoner's Dilemmas — each firm is tempted to undercut — which is why price-fixing is unstable and why leniency programs (rewarding the first defector) work.
**Evolution:** Hawk-Dove's mixed equilibrium predicts the observed ratio of aggressive to passive individuals; the Prisoner's Dilemma underlies the evolution of reciprocal altruism.
**International relations:** arms races are Prisoner's Dilemmas; brinkmanship crises are Chicken; alliance coordination is a Stag Hunt.
**Environmental policy:** emissions and overfishing are multiplayer Prisoner's Dilemmas ('tragedy of the commons') needing binding agreements.
**Sports & security:** penalty kicks, serve direction, and randomized inspection are zero-sum / Matching-Pennies problems solved by mixing.

problems.prompt:
Prisoner's Dilemma. Two suspects each choose Cooperate (stay silent) or Defect (confess). Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff); higher is better.

| Row \ Col | Cooperate | Defect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooperate** | 3, 3 | 0, 5 |
| **Defect** | 5, 0 | 1, 1 |

steps.instruction: If Column Cooperates, Row compares Cooperate (3) vs Defect (5). Row's best response? Choose Cooperate or Defect.
Each player's strictly dominant strategy is? Choose Cooperate or Defect.
steps.hint: Defect beats Cooperate in BOTH columns.

problems.solutionNarrative: Because 5 > 3 and 1 > 0, Defect strictly dominates for both. The dominant-strategy equilibrium is (Defect, Defect) at (1,1) — worse than mutual cooperation (3,3).

problems.prompt:
Prisoner's Dilemma. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Cooperate | Defect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooperate** | 4, 4 | 0, 6 |
| **Defect** | 6, 0 | 2, 2 |

steps.instruction: What is the Nash-equilibrium outcome? Enter Row's strategy: Cooperate or Defect.
steps.hint: Both play their dominant strategy.

steps.instruction: At (Defect, Defect) each earns 2; at (Cooperate, Cooperate) each earns 4. Which mutual outcome is better for both? Type 'Cooperate' or 'Defect'.
steps.hint: Compare 4 with 2.

steps.instruction: How much MORE would each earn under mutual cooperation than the equilibrium? (Give a number.)

problems.solutionNarrative: Equilibrium (Defect, Defect) pays 2 each, but (Cooperate, Cooperate) pays 4 — a Pareto improvement of 2 per player that self-interest throws away.

problems.prompt:
Price war (a Prisoner's Dilemma). Two firms choose Price High (collude) or Price Low (compete). Payoffs are profits (Row's, Column's).

| Row \ Col | Price High | Price Low |
|---|---|---|
| **Price High** | 5, 5 | 1, 8 |
| **Price Low** | 8, 1 | 3, 3 |

steps.instruction: Is Price Low a strictly dominant strategy for each firm? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Compare Low vs High in each column: 8>5 and 3>1.

steps.instruction: Nash-equilibrium outcome — enter Row's strategy: Price High or Price Low.
steps.hint: Both defect to the low price.

steps.instruction: Collusive profit is 5; equilibrium profit is 3. How much profit does each firm LOSE by competing? (Give a number.)

problems.solutionNarrative: Price Low dominates, so the equilibrium is (Price Low, Price Low) at 3 each — below the 5 of collusion. Competition destroys 2 profit per firm, which is why cartels are unstable.

problems.prompt:
Stag Hunt. Two hunters can jointly hunt a Stag (big reward, needs both) or individually catch a Hare (small, safe). Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Stag | Hare |
|---|---|---|
| **Stag** | 5, 5 | 0, 3 |
| **Hare** | 3, 0 | 3, 3 |

steps.instruction: Is (Stag, Stag) a Nash equilibrium? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: 5 vs deviating to Hare (3) — nobody gains by switching.

steps.instruction: Is (Hare, Hare) a Nash equilibrium? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: If the other hunts Hare, Stag alone earns 0.

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Both diagonal outcomes are self-enforcing.

problems.solutionNarrative: (Stag, Stag) — payoff-dominant at 5 each — and (Hare, Hare) — risk-dominant at 3 each — are both Nash. Coordination turns on trust.

problems.prompt:
Battle of the Sexes. A couple wants to be together; Row prefers Ballet, Column prefers Football. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Ballet | Football |
|---|---|---|
| **Ballet** | 3, 2 | 0, 0 |
| **Football** | 0, 0 | 2, 3 |

steps.instruction: At the equilibrium Row prefers, both attend which event? Type 'Ballet' or 'Football'.
steps.hint: Row's favorite gives Row 3.

steps.instruction: At the equilibrium Column prefers, both attend which event? Type 'Ballet' or 'Football'.
steps.hint: Column's favorite gives Column 3.

steps.instruction: Total number of pure Nash equilibria? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Both 'together' outcomes qualify.

problems.solutionNarrative: (Ballet, Ballet) and (Football, Football) are both Nash — being together beats the (0,0) of being apart. The players agree to coordinate but disagree on which equilibrium.

problems.prompt:
Stag Hunt (trust and risk). Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Stag | Hare |
|---|---|---|
| **Stag** | 6, 6 | 0, 3 |
| **Hare** | 3, 0 | 3, 3 |

steps.instruction: Which equilibrium gives BOTH players the highest payoff (payoff-dominant)? Type 'Stag' or 'Hare'.
steps.hint: 6 each vs 3 each.

steps.instruction: Which single strategy guarantees at least 3 no matter what the other does (risk-dominant)? Type 'Stag' or 'Hare'.
steps.hint: Stag risks earning 0 if the partner defects.

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria are there? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Both diagonal cells.

problems.solutionNarrative: (Stag, Stag) is payoff-dominant (6 each) but risky; Hare is risk-dominant, guaranteeing 3. Reaching the better equilibrium depends on trust.

problems.prompt:
Hawk-Dove. Two animals contest a resource worth V = 4. Fighting (Hawk vs Hawk) costs C = 6, so each earns (V-C)/2 = -1. A Hawk facing a Dove takes it all (4); two Doves share (2 each). Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Hawk | Dove |
|---|---|---|
| **Hawk** | -1, -1 | 4, 0 |
| **Dove** | 0, 4 | 2, 2 |

steps.instruction: If Column plays Hawk, is Row's best response Dove? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Dove earns 0, Hawk earns -1 — 0 is better.

steps.instruction: If Column plays Dove, is Row's best response Hawk? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Hawk takes 4 vs sharing 2.

problems.solutionNarrative: Each animal wants the OPPOSITE of its rival: Hawk against a Dove, Dove against a Hawk. Anti-coordination gives two off-diagonal equilibria, (Hawk, Dove) and (Dove, Hawk).

problems.prompt:
Chicken. Two drivers race head-on. Straight vs a swerving rival wins (4); both Straight crash (-2 each); both Swerve is mild (1 each). Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Swerve | Straight |
|---|---|---|
| **Swerve** | 1, 1 | 0, 4 |
| **Straight** | 4, 0 | -2, -2 |

steps.instruction: Is (Straight, Straight) a Nash equilibrium? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: From a crash (-2), either would rather have swerved (0).

steps.instruction: Is (Swerve, Straight) a Nash equilibrium? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Row swerving gets 0 (deviating to Straight crashes); Column straight gets 4.

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria does Chicken have? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: The two off-diagonal outcomes.

problems.solutionNarrative: The equilibria are the off-diagonal cells: exactly one driver yields. The mutual-Straight crash shows why brinkmanship is dangerous.

problems.prompt:
Hawk-Dove. V = 6, C = 10; Hawk-Hawk yields (V-C)/2 = -2 each. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Hawk | Dove |
|---|---|---|
| **Hawk** | -2, -2 | 6, 0 |
| **Dove** | 0, 6 | 3, 3 |

steps.instruction: List a pure Nash equilibrium — Row's strategy where Row is aggressive. Type 'Hawk' or 'Dove'.
steps.hint: (Hawk, Dove): Row Hawk takes 6.

steps.instruction: At that (Hawk, Dove) equilibrium, what is Row's payoff? (Give a number.)
steps.hint: A Hawk facing a Dove takes the whole resource.

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria are there? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: (Hawk, Dove) and (Dove, Hawk).

problems.solutionNarrative: The pure equilibria are (Hawk, Dove) and (Dove, Hawk); the Hawk takes V = 6 while the Dove gets 0. A stable population mixes the two — the mixed equilibrium of evolutionary game theory.

problems.prompt:
Matching Pennies (zero-sum). Row wins (+1) on a match, Column wins (+1) on a mismatch. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Heads | Tails |
|---|---|---|
| **Heads** | 1, -1 | -1, 1 |
| **Tails** | -1, 1 | 1, -1 |

steps.instruction: Does this game have a pure Nash equilibrium? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Someone always wants to switch.

steps.instruction: In the mixed equilibrium, with what probability does Row play Heads? Give a fraction or decimal.
steps.hint: By symmetry the two options are equally likely.

problems.solutionNarrative: No pure equilibrium exists; the unique equilibrium is mixed, each player choosing Heads and Tails with probability 1/2. Any bias would be exploited.

problems.prompt:
A symmetric zero-sum matching game with stakes 3. Row wins +3 on a match, Column wins +3 on a mismatch. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Heads | Tails |
|---|---|---|
| **Heads** | 3, -3 | -3, 3 |
| **Tails** | -3, 3 | 3, -3 |

steps.instruction: Is the sum of the two players' payoffs zero in every cell? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Add the two numbers in each cell.

steps.instruction: Equilibrium probability that Row plays Heads? Fraction or decimal.
steps.hint: Symmetry forces 50/50.

steps.instruction: Row's expected payoff at the equilibrium (a fair game). (Give a number.)
steps.hint: Wins and losses cancel by symmetry.

problems.solutionNarrative: Every cell sums to 0 (zero-sum). The equilibrium mix is 1/2–1/2, giving each an expected payoff of 0 — the value of a symmetric zero-sum game.

problems.prompt:
A zero-sum matching game with asymmetric stakes. Row scores 3 on (Heads, Heads) and 1 on (Tails, Tails), 0 otherwise; Column's payoff is the negative. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Heads | Tails |
|---|---|---|
| **Heads** | 3, -3 | 0, 0 |
| **Tails** | 0, 0 | 1, -1 |

steps.instruction: Does a pure Nash equilibrium exist? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Row wants to match, Column to mismatch — a cycle.

steps.instruction: Let q = prob Column plays Heads. Row is indifferent when 3q = 1 - q. Solve for q. Fraction or decimal in (0,1).
Because the (Heads, Heads) win of 3 is larger, does Column play Heads with probability MORE or LESS than 1/2? Type 'more' or 'less'.
steps.hint: Compare 1/4 with 1/2.

problems.solutionNarrative: Solving 3q = 1 - q gives q = 1/4. Asymmetric stakes tilt the mix away from 50/50: the bigger payoff cell is defended less often so the opponent stays indifferent.

problems.prompt:
Classify the game below by its equilibria. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Left | Right |
|---|---|---|
| **Top** | 2, 2 | 0, 0 |
| **Bottom** | 0, 0 | 1, 1 |

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria are there? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Both diagonal cells are mutual best responses.

steps.instruction: Is this a coordination game (reward matching) or a Prisoner's Dilemma (dominant defection)? Type 'coordination' or 'dilemma'.
steps.hint: Players do best by choosing the same option.

problems.solutionNarrative: Two diagonal equilibria (Top, Left) and (Bottom, Right) where players match — this is a coordination game, not a Prisoner's Dilemma.

problems.prompt:
Two nations choose Disarm or Arm. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | Disarm | Arm |
|---|---|---|
| **Disarm** | 3, 3 | 0, 5 |
| **Arm** | 5, 0 | 1, 1 |

steps.instruction: Each nation's dominant strategy is? Choose Disarm or Arm.
steps.hint: Arm beats Disarm in both columns (5>3, 1>0).

steps.instruction: The equilibrium (Arm, Arm) pays 1 each; (Disarm, Disarm) pays 3 each. Is (Disarm, Disarm) better for both? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: Compare 3 with 1.

problems.solutionNarrative: Arm strictly dominates, so the equilibrium is (Arm, Arm) at (1,1) — both worse off than the (3,3) of mutual disarmament. This arms race is a Prisoner's Dilemma.

problems.prompt:
Distinguish the archetypes. Each cell is (Row's payoff, Column's payoff).

| Row \ Col | A | B |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0, 0 | 3, 1 |
| **B** | 1, 3 | 0, 0 |

steps.instruction: Do the players do best by matching (both A or both B) or by mismatching (one A, one B)? Type 'matching' or 'mismatching'.
steps.hint: The nonzero payoffs are off the diagonal.

steps.instruction: How many pure Nash equilibria are there? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: The two off-diagonal cells.

problems.solutionNarrative: Payoffs reward mismatching, so this is an anti-coordination game with two off-diagonal equilibria (A, B) and (B, A) — the Chicken/Hawk-Dove pattern.

problems.prompt:
For each game state how many pure Nash equilibria it has. Game 1 (left) and Game 2 (right).

Game 1:

| Row \ Col | Stag | Hare |
|---|---|---|
| **Stag** | 4, 4 | 0, 2 |
| **Hare** | 2, 0 | 2, 2 |

Game 2:

| Row \ Col | Heads | Tails |
|---|---|---|
| **Heads** | 1, -1 | -1, 1 |
| **Tails** | -1, 1 | 1, -1 |

steps.instruction: Game 1 (Stag Hunt): how many pure Nash equilibria? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Both diagonal cells are self-enforcing.

steps.instruction: Game 2 (Matching Pennies): how many pure Nash equilibria? (Give an integer.)
steps.hint: Best responses cycle; none is stable.

steps.instruction: Which game requires a mixed strategy to have any equilibrium? Type 'Game 1' or 'Game 2'.
steps.hint: The one with zero pure equilibria.

problems.solutionNarrative: The Stag Hunt has 2 pure equilibria (matching); Matching Pennies has 0 and needs a 1/2–1/2 mixed equilibrium. Counting pure equilibria (2 vs 0) is how you tell coordination from zero-sum.
